exemplar.title: How do varying temperatures, 15, 25, 35, 45 and 55C, affect the viscosity of Acacia Honey, as measured through a falling ball viscometer experiment and recorded through digital photo analysis?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do varying temperatures, 15, 25, 35, 45 and 55C, affect the viscosity of Acacia Honey, as measured through a falling ball viscometer experiment and recorded through digital photo analysis?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data recording, tables and graphs are communicated with clarity and precision (correct units, significant figures, error bars)
Uncertainties are calculated and propagated appropriately, with both random and systematic sources addressed
Processing steps (velocity extraction, outlier treatment, Stokesâ law application, exponential fitting) are mathematically sound and logically sequenced
weaknesses: Inconsistent unit usage (e.g., g in cm/sÂ² rather than SI), missing wall-correction for Stokes flow
Typographical symbol mix-ups (viscosity denoted as ânâ instead of Î·) and slight inconsistencies in significant figures
Error-propagation formulas quoted are not strictly correctâcould be improved with full differential treatment
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the descriptor of the top band: data recording is both clear and precise, consideration of uncertainties is appropriate, and processing is carried out accurately. Minor slips do not materially affect the validity of the analysis. The studentâs work exemplifies the 5â6 band, meriting the maximum 6 points.

strengths: Well-focused, contextualised research question specifying temperature range and honey type
Comprehensive apparatus list, step-by-step procedure and annotated diagram enabling reproducibility
Identification and control of multiple variables (e.g., marble density/diameter, release height, stirring technique)
weaknesses: Some procedural details remain ambiguous (e.g., âreasonableâ camera distance, frame-skipping settings not fully specified)
Apparatus list omits camera specifications (resolution, frame rate)
Honey brand and purity are not stated, limiting full reproducibility
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs research question is situated in a very clear industrial context and the falling-ball viscometer method is justified (band 5â6), and the methodology description allows replication albeit with minor omissions (band 5). Methodological considerations are described but not fully explained in terms of data sufficiency (band 3â4). Overall performance lies at the top of the 3â4 band but falls short of flawless explanation in one strand, warranting 5/6.

strengths: Conclusion is directly justified by the processed data, restating the exponential decrease and citing the numerical fit and RÂ²
Strong consistency between analysis and stated outcome, fully addressing the original hypothesis
Results are compared to published honey viscosity data with reasoned discussion of divergence (humidity, sugar content, altitude)
weaknesses: No quantitative percent-difference or activation-energy comparison to deepen the context
Comparison could be strengthened by relating extracted Arrhenius parameters to literature values
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully consistent withâand justified byâthe analytical results (band 5â6). The scientific context comparison goes beyond superficial treatment by plotting and discussing divergences, satisfying the top-band descriptor. While further quantitative depth could improve it, the current level clearly falls within 5â6, justifying 6/6.

strengths: Identifies multiple specific methodological weaknesses and explains how each could have affected data (e.g., temperature gradients, frame-rate variability, contamination)
Proposes realistic, relevant improvements with explanation of their impact (e.g., fixed release guide to reduce systematic error, uniform frame advance setting)
Demonstrates an understanding of how methodological changes would reduce uncertainty and improve accuracy
weaknesses: Suggested improvements lack quantitative estimates of uncertainty reduction (e.g., gradient variance before/after stirring modification)
Could further prioritise which improvements yield the largest reduction in overall uncertainty
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student not only describes specific weaknesses but also explains their relative impact on results and offers realistic, relevant improvements with rationaleâmeeting all aspects of the 5â6 band. Although numerical quantification of uncertainty reduction is missing, the explanations sufficiently demonstrate high-level evaluation, meriting 6/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a focused research question that specifies both the range of temperatures (15â55 Â°C) and the substance (acacia honey) to be investigated with a falling-ball viscometer.  In the introduction the importance of viscosity in industry and lubrication is explicitly linked to the chosen system, demonstrating the particular relevance of honey and justifying why a falling-ball method combined with digital video analysis is suitable.  This places the question in a clear, specific and appropriate scientific context, fully satisfying the top-band descriptor.

Uncertainties are provided for every measured quantity, random uncertainties are calculated with maxâmin/2, and propagation is performed to obtain viscosity uncertainties.  Graphs display error bars and the discussion acknowledges an outlier through a box-plot test.  Systematic sources (e.g., wall effects, Stokes-flow assumptions) are only touched on indirectly, so the treatment is strong but not exhaustive.  This aligns with âappropriate considerationâ though not exemplary, so a score of 5 within the 5â6 band is assigned.

The student identifies several variables (density and diameter of marble, release height, camera geometry, honey type, stirring technique) and explains, at least briefly, how each is controlled and why control is necessary.  Safety, ethical and environmental considerations are also presented.  While these considerations are relevant and generally sufficient, the explanations of how each control ensures collection of *sufficient* data (e.g., justification of five temperatures, rationale for number of trials) are only implicit.  The coverage meets the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, warranting the upper end of the 3â4 band.

Three specific weaknesses are identified, and for each the student describes the way it could have affected the data (temperature gradients causing different viscosities at depth, camera-frame settings altering precision, contamination).  The relative *impact* (which error dominates the overall uncertainty) is not analysed, so the explanation remains descriptive rather than analytical.  This aligns with the 3â4 band at its top end.

The apparatus list, step-by-step procedure (items 1â25) and annotated diagram give enough detail for another student to replicate the investigation.  Key dimensions (drop height 30.4 cm, marble radius 0.79 cm, honey mass, water-bath geometry) and the way videos are captured/analysed are all stated.  Minor ambiguities remain (exact camera-to-cylinder distance is described as âreasonableâ, and the frame-skipping setting is temperature-dependent without precise values), so the description is marginally short of flawless, but still clearly within the 5â6 band.  A score of 5 is therefore appropriate.

Processing stepsâgradient extraction for terminal velocity, removal of an identified outlier, application of Stokesâ law, and full uncertainty propagationâare mathematically correct and logically sequenced.  The exponential fit is appropriate and the correlation coefficient is quoted.  Minor slips (typographical mix-ups of symbols, no wall-correction factor) do not materially affect the final values.  The work therefore meets the descriptor of âappropriate and accurateâ, meriting 5 points.

For every cited weakness a realistic, feasible improvement is provided (longer stirring rod, full water bath, uniform frame rate, sanitising tools).  The suggestions are relevant and include some rationale, but they are not elaborated in depth (e.g., no quantitative estimate of improvement).  This satisfies âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ improvements, warranting 4 marks in the 3â4 band.

Data tables include headings with correct units, significant figures and random uncertainties; processed values are presented to consistent precision.  Graphs are labelled, include error bars and fitted functions, and captions explain their purpose.  Explanatory text clearly links the raw displacementâtime plots to calculated velocities and viscosities.  Both clarity (easy to follow) and precision (correct units, symbols, significant figures) are consistently demonstrated, fully satisfying the top-band descriptor.

The student compares results with published data for Indian honeys, plots both sets on one graph and discusses possible reasons for divergence (humidity, sugar content, altitude).  This is certainly ârelevant comparisonâ, but the discussion lacks quantitative depth (no calculation of percentage difference or activation energy comparison).  Hence the work reaches the upper level of the 3â4 band.

The conclusion clearly restates the observed exponential decrease of viscosity with temperature and cites the numerical fit and correlation coefficient as evidence.  It directly addresses the hypothesis and is consistent with processed data.  While convincing, the explanation could be tighter on quantitative agreement (e.g., activation energy), so a strong but not perfect performance justifies 5 marks within the top band.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the research in an industrial context but could benefit from citing specific examples or data to substantiate the significance of viscosity in lubrication applications. Suggest including numerical references for real-world relevance.

The research question is precise and contextualized, but consider specifying the honeyâs brand and purity to ensure reproducibility. Including these details strengthens the clarity of the experimental design.
The Stokes drag equation is missing the velocity term. It should read $$F_d = 6\pi \eta r v$$. Including $$v$$ is critical for accurate derivation of viscosity.
The caption erroneously refers to viscosity as ânâ instead of the standard Greek letter $$\eta$$. Consistent notation is vital for clear communication of equations.

Referring to the Arrhenius relationship as a ânatural exponent curveâ omits discussion of the linear form $$\ln\eta\) vs. \(1/T$$. Including this transformation would strengthen the theoretical treatment.

Using data for Indian honeys to predict Acacia honey viscosities requires justification of compositional similarity. Discuss differences in sugar content and moisture that could affect extrapolation accuracy.

The hypothesis correctly predicts an exponential decrease in viscosity, but could be enhanced by specifying expected quantitative parameters (e.g., activation energy) to link with the Arrhenius model.
Describing camera distance as âsame distanceâ lacks precision. Specify the measured distance and angle (e.g., 40 cm, perpendicular alignment) to improve reproducibility.
Releasing the marble âat eye levelâ introduces operator variability. Recommend using a fixed guide or mount to ensure consistent release height to reduce systematic error.
The stirring protocol (every 5â10 min) is vague. Define a fixed interval (e.g., every 5 min precisely) with timing device to ensure uniform mixing and temperature distribution.
The hazard mitigation is adequate but could address handling of thermometers in hot liquids (e.g., use heat-resistant gloves). This ensures full coverage of safety considerations.
The apparatus list omits camera specifications (resolution, frame rate) which are critical for digital analysis accuracy. Include these parameters to enable replication.
The setup image is clear, but a scale bar or dimension annotations (e.g., cylinder height) would improve comprehension and allow precise experimental reconstruction.
The procedure headings and step numbers jump, causing potential confusion. Ensure consecutive numbering and clear separation of subsection procedures for readability.
Only the 15Â°C case is detailed for frame-skipping. Provide explicit settings for each temperature or justify changing the frame advance to maintain consistent temporal resolution.
The concept of instantaneous versus terminal velocity is introduced well, but consider adding the criterion (e.g., standard deviation threshold) used to identify when velocity becomes constant.
âReasonable distanceâ is subjective. Provide a numeric distance value (e.g., â40 cm from cameraâ) to ensure consistent framing and reduce parallax errors.
The graph lacks error bars on displacement points which would indicate measurement uncertainty. Including error bars strengthens the reliability of the velocity gradient extraction.
The rationale for taking absolute velocities is clear, but explicitly stating the sign convention at the start of calculations would improve clarity and avoid potential confusion.
Terminal velocities are given with varying decimal places. Standardize to consistent significant figures (e.g., three significant figures) to maintain precision and clarity.
The box-plot method for outlier detection is appropriate, but include criteria (e.g., 1.5ÃIQR rule) in the text to document the decision threshold.
Using g = 981 cm sâ»Â² is unconventional; SI units use g = 9.81 m sâ»Â². Convert all measurements into SI units consistently to avoid unit conversion errors.
The summation notation $$\Sigma F_g + F_b + F_d = 0$$ is correct, but adding subscripts (e.g., $$\Sigma_i F_i=0$$) clarifies the vector nature and avoids sign ambiguity.

The max/min formulas $$\max\eta = \frac{\max - \min}{\min}$$ and $$\min\eta = \dots$$ are incorrect for error propagation. Use differential propagation $$\delta\eta = \sqrt{\sum (\frac{\partial\eta}{\partial x_i}\delta x_i)^2}$$ for accuracy.
The additional variables table is thorough, but could include systematic uncertainty contributions (e.g., wall effect, temperature gradient) to present a complete uncertainty budget.
The citation style varies across entries. Adopt a consistent format (e.g., APA or MLA) and include DOIs where available to enhance academic rigor.
The random uncertainties are large relative to measured viscosities at high temperatures. Discuss how these uncertainties affect the reliability of the exponential fit in Figure 7.
The graph reports $$R^2=0.9997$$ but lacks error bars on data points. Including error bars would demonstrate whether the fit falls within experimental uncertainty.

The discussion identifies environmental factors qualitatively; include a quantitative percent difference analysis (e.g., $$\%\,\text{difference}=\frac{|\eta_{\rm exp}-\eta_{\rm pred}|}{\eta_{\rm pred}}$$) to substantiate claims.
The overlay of estimated vs. experimental viscosities is insightful, but annotate which symbols correspond to each dataset and add error bars for clarity.
The conclusion restates findings effectively, but could be strengthened by relating the activation energy parameter extracted from the fit to values in literature for honey systems.

The proposed improvements are relevant but descriptive. Explain how each suggestion quantitatively reduces uncertainty (e.g., reducing stirring gradients could lower temperature variance from Â±3Â°C to Â±1Â°C).
While using five trials meets IB recommendations, discuss whether this number sufficiently reduced random uncertainty (e.g., show uncertainty vs. number of trials to justify).
Good identification of temperature gradient issues; consider implementing a circulating water bath with stirrer to maintain isothermal conditions and quantify gradient reduction.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
